attributes.stem:
A 57-year-old woman is admitted to a small rural hospital with a two-day history of constant right upper quadrant pain. The pain began after eating a big meal of fish and chips, and has not resolved with simple analgesia since. She has never had any pain like this before. She reports nausea but no vomiting, fever, or jaundice. She has a medical history of appendicectomy and occasional gastro-oesophageal reflux when drinking alcohol. She does not take any regular medications.

On examination, she appears uncomfortable. Her pulse rate is 90/min, blood pressure 124/76 mmHg, respiratory rate 18/min, SpO2 98% on room air, and temperature 37.3°C. Her BMI is 25 kg/m². Chest examination is unremarkable. Abdominal examination is unremarkable apart from when the examining hand is placed under the right costal margin and causes pain and inspiratory arrest when the patient is asked to breathe in deeply.

Investigations reveal:

  • Haemoglobin: 135 g/L (115-160)

  • White cell count: 9.8 × 10⁹/L (4.0-11.0)

  • Platelets: 250 × 10⁹/L (150-400)

  • C-reactive protein: 45 mg/L (<5)

  • Bilirubin: 15 μmol/L (<20)

  • Alanine aminotransferase: 35 U/L (<35)

  • Alkaline phosphatase: 95 U/L (30-110)

  • Gamma-glutamyl transferase: 38 U/L (<40)

An ultrasound reveals a gallstone impacted in the cystic duct and a thickened gallbladder wall measuring 5 mm.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate management?
options: Conservative management with analgesia and observation
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with clinical features of acute cholecystitis, confirmed by ultrasound findings of a gallstone impacted in the cystic duct and gallbladder wall thickening. The management of acute cholecystitis depends on the patient's fitness for surgery, timing of presentation, and available resources.

Early laparoscopic cholecystectomy is the preferred management for acute cholecystitis in patients who are fit for surgery and present within 72 hours of symptom onset. Early cholecystectomy (within 72 hours) has been shown to reduce overall hospital stay, treatment costs, and risk of recurrent gallstone-related complications compared to delayed surgery. This patient is relatively young, has minimal comorbidities, and presents within the optimal timeframe for early intervention.

Intravenous antibiotics and interval cholecystectomy (delayed cholecystectomy after 6-8 weeks) would be appropriate for patients with significant comorbidities making them high-risk for immediate surgery, or in settings where surgical expertise is not immediately available. While this is a rural hospital, the question does not indicate limitations in surgical capability, and the patient appears to be a good candidate for early surgery.

Percutaneous cholecystostomy is typically reserved for patients who are too unwell for surgery or have failed to respond to conservative management. It involves drainage of the gallbladder under radiological guidance. This patient does not have features suggesting severe sepsis or organ dysfunction that would necessitate this approach as first-line management.

Endoscopic retrograde cholangiopancreatography would be indicated if there were evidence of common bile duct stones or cholangitis, such as jaundice, significantly deranged liver function tests, or dilated bile ducts on imaging. The patient has normal liver function tests and no clinical features suggesting common bile duct obstruction.

Conservative management with analgesia and observation alone without antibiotics would be inadequate for confirmed acute cholecystitis. While mild cases might be managed conservatively in very specific circumstances, the presence of an impacted stone, gallbladder wall thickening, and elevated C-reactive protein indicates established inflammation requiring more definitive management.

The Tokyo Guidelines for acute cholecystitis provide a severity grading system and management algorithm. For Grade I (mild) cholecystitis in a patient fit for surgery, early laparoscopic cholecystectomy is recommended. This patient's presentation is consistent with Grade I cholecystitis, making early surgery the most appropriate option.
learningPoints: Early laparoscopic cholecystectomy is the preferred management for acute cholecystitis in fit patients presenting within 72 hours of symptom onset.
Conservative management with antibiotics is appropriate for patients with significant comorbidities or delayed presentation.
Ultrasound findings of gallstone impaction in the cystic duct with gallbladder wall thickening are diagnostic of acute cholecystitis.
references: Crawford M. Biliary pain--work-up and management in general practice. Aust Fam Physician. 2013 Jul;42(7):458-61. Available at: https://www.racgp.org.au/afp/2013/july/biliary-pain
